---
title: "Command line interface"
---

This document describes the command-line interface (CLI) as a development
reference and to track implementation status.

{{< include /docs/includes/_design-status.qmd >}}

## {{< var planned >}} `seedcase-propagate`

`seedcase-propagate` has the following signature:

```bash {filename="Terminal"}
seedcase-propagate <COMMAND> [PARAMETERS]
```

When used on its own, `seedcase-propagate` shows the help message as a shorthand
for `--help`:

```bash {filename="Terminal"}
seedcase-propagate
```

::: callout-note
As a convenience, we provide the command `propagate` as a shorthand for
`seedcase-propagate`. It can be used in place of `seedcase-propagate` in all
instances.
:::

`seedcase-propagate` includes common CLI commands like `version` and `help`
(which is the same output as `seedcase-propagate --help`) as well as three
Propagate commands: `build`, `subset`, and `request`.

## {{< var planned >}} `seedcase-propagate build`

<!-- TODO: Call it `METADATA` or `SOURCE`? `SOURCE` was used in Flower, but should that be the same here?  -->

The `build` command builds the HTML and WebAssembly (WASM) files for the
client-side web application that can be inserted into static websites (described
in [outputs](outputs.qmd)) from the metadata (source) in the `datapackage.json`
file. It has the following signature that is positional as well as
keyword-based:

```bash {filename="Terminal"}
seedcase-propagate build [SOURCE] [OUTPUT-DIR]
```

<!-- TODO: Do we include a config? And if so, what would it have? -->
<!-- TODO: Do we include theming capability? -->
<!-- TODO: Include other default metadata files to search for? -->

The default for `OUTPUT-DIR` is `./docs/request/` and for `SOURCE` is
`./datapackage.json`. The diagram below shows the flow of inputs and outputs for
`build`:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-cli-build-flow
%%| fig-cap: "The flow of input and output through the CLI `build` command."
%%| fig-alt: "Diagram showing the flow of input and output through the CLI `build` command. The user provides the source of the metadata file and output directory for the files."

flowchart LR
    source[/"datapackage.json<br>[SOURCE: file, https,<br>gh/github]"/]
    build["build"]
    output_dir[/"./docs/request/<br>[OUTPUT-DIR: folder]"/]
    output[/"Output<br>[HTML and<br>WASM files]"/]

    source --> build
    output_dir --> build
    build --> output
```

The main argument to `build` is the location of the metadata file as a `SOURCE`
(e.g. a filepath or a URL) to build the documentation from. Propagate supports
the following sources:

- {{< var planned >}} `file://` or a path without `file://`
- {{< var planned >}} `https://`
- {{< var planned >}} `gh:` or `github:`

`build`'s behaviour depends on the `SOURCE` format given:

- If nothing is given, `build` by default assumes that `source` is a filepath to
  a common metadata file such as `datapackage.json` in the current working
  directory, e.g. it will look for `./datapackage.json`.
- If a relative path to a folder is given, `build` will parse it to the absolute
  path and append the metadata file, e.g. `datapackage.json`, so that the path
  becomes `/ABSOLUTE_PATH/datapackage.json`.
- `https://` sources must point to a raw file. We won't support using `http://`
  for security reasons.
- `gh:` or `github:` sources must be in the format `gh:OWNER/REPO` or
  `github:OWNER/REPO`. It's possible to add `@REF` to specify a Git ref, like a
  branch name, tag, or commit hash. `build` will parse this source to the raw
  `https:` of some common metadata file formats (e.g. `datapackage.json`), which
  ever returns a real URL. For example:
  - `gh:seedcase-project/seedcase-flower` will parse to
    `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle/main/datapackage.json`.
  - `github:seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle@0.2.0` will parse to
    `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle/0.2.0/datapackage.json`.
  - `gh:seedcase-project/seedcase-flower@main` will parse to
    `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seedcase-project/example-seed-beetle/main/datapackage.json`.

## {{< var planned >}} `seedcase-propagate subset`

<!-- TODO: Should instructions include the name of the output file(s)? -->

The `subset` command takes four arguments: an instructions
[`request.toml`](inputs.qmd) file for what to subset, the data package's
metadata file (e.g. `datapackage.json`), the output directory, and a plan flag.
It outputs a data file or files as Parquet, with metadata from the metadata file
(e.g. `datapackage.json`) embedded into the metadata of the Parquet file(s). It
has the following signature that is positional as well as keyword-based:

```bash {filename="Terminal"}
seedcase-propagate subset [REQUEST] [SOURCE] [OUTPUT-DIR] [SHOW-PLAN]
```

The only required argument is `REQUEST`, which is the path to the `request.toml`
file that contains the instructions on what to subset. That's because the
`subset` command is meant to be run by the data owner or data manager while in
the data package folder, which means that the metadata file (e.g.
`datapackage.json`) is likely in the same folder.

The `SOURCE` argument is the same as the `SOURCE` argument used above for
`build`. The default for `OUTPUT-DIR` is `./subsets/`. The name of the output
file(s) will be determined either from:

- The request file's `name` field and the source file's `name` field (e.g.
  `name` field in `datapackage.json`).
- Just the request file's `name` field if the metadata does not contain some
  type of `name` field.

The `SHOW-PLAN` flag is a boolean that defaults to `false`. If `true`, the CLI
will output a plan of what the `subset` command will do to create the subset.
The output is a SQL set of commands, because SQL is the language of data and
will be likely understood by most users. This is useful for users to check what
set of commands the subset will run when creating the subset.

The diagram below shows the flow of inputs and outputs for `subset`:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-cli-subset-flow
%%| fig-cap: "The flow of input and output through the CLI `subset` command."
%%| fig-alt: "Diagram showing the flow of input and output through the CLI `subset` command. The user provides the source of the metadata file, the request file, and output directory for the files."

flowchart LR
    source[/"datapackage.json<br>[SOURCE: file, https,<br>gh/github]"/]
    subset["subset"]
    request[/"request.toml<br>[REQUEST: request.toml<br>instructions for subsetting]"/]
    show_plan[/"--show-plan<br>[SHOW-PLAN: flag to output SQL plan]"/]
    output_dir[/"./subsets/<br>[OUTPUT-DIR: folder]"/]
    output[/"./subsets/*.parquet<br>[Parquet file(s)]"/]

    request --> subset
    source --> subset
    output_dir --> subset
    show_plan --> subset
    subset --> output
```

## {{< var planned >}} `seedcase-propagate request`

The `request` command takes a source metadata file and opens up a terminal user
interface to interactively create a `request.toml` file from the metadata. It
essentially is a terminal-based version of the web application that is built by
the `build` command. It has the following signature that is positional as well
as keyword-based:

```bash {filename="Terminal"}
seedcase-propagate request [SOURCE]
```

Because of the nature of using an interactive terminal user interface, the
default file created from the `request` command is `./request.toml` in the
current working directory. The user is then responsible for sending the request
file to the data owner or data manager to create the subset.

The diagram below shows the flow of inputs and outputs for `request`:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-cli-request-flow
%%| fig-cap: "The flow of input and output through the CLI `request` command."
%%| fig-alt: "Diagram showing the flow of input and output through the CLI `request` command. The user only needs to provide the source of the metadata file."

flowchart LR
    source[/"datapackage.json<br>[SOURCE: file, https,<br>gh/github]"/]
    request["request"]
    tui["Terminal user interface<br>[Interactive]"]
    output[/"./request.toml<br>[Instructions file]"/]

    source --> request
    request --> tui
    tui --> output
```
